headline: Boston College's ground game overwhelms Colorado in lopsided Week 3 affair
body: Boston College's offense dictated terms from the opening drive, establishing a 126-yard rushing advantage that Colorado's defense simply could not match. The Eagles controlled the line of scrimmage throughout the afternoon, forcing Colorado into predictable passing situations and preventing the Buffaloes from establishing any sustained rhythm on offense. Quarterback Leavitt distributed the ball efficiently across 27 completions, finding open receivers downfield while the run game kept chains moving and the clock ticking in Boston College's favor.

Colorado's passing attack, led by Anderson's 18-for-43 effort, revealed the cost of playing from behind. The Buffaloes managed two touchdown passes but operated in reactive mode for most of the game, unable to generate the yardage or efficiency needed to keep pace. Van Horne's three carries for six yards underscored the reality: Colorado's running back could not find creases, and that inability to threaten on the ground left Anderson and the passing game isolated and vulnerable to Boston College's pass rush.

The Eagles' defensive front, anchored by Collins' two sacks, made life uncomfortable for Anderson throughout. Boston College surrendered nothing in the turnover column while Boston College similarly protected the football, a clean exchange that favored the more dominant team. This was control football executed at a level Colorado's staff could not match or answer, a Week 3 statement from a Boston College team that appears to have found its identity.
turningPoint: Boston College's opening-quarter establishment of the run game forced Colorado into obvious passing situations, where the Buffaloes' defense became vulnerable to Leavitt's intermediate throws and the Eagles' play-action offense.

unsungHero.name: Mcdonald
unsungHero.why: His 84 yards on 13 carries provided the grinding, consistent yardage that sustained drives and kept Colorado's defense honest, even though MacCormick's single explosive reception stole the Player of the Game honor.
